coredata.dc:title: Self-assembled zinc phthalocyanine nanoparticles as excellent photothermal/photodynamic synergistic agent for antitumor treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhao
Gai, Shili
Wang, Chuanqing
Yang, Guixin
Zhong, Chongna
Dai, Yunlu
He, Fei
Yang, Dan
Yang, Piaoping
coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional phototheranostic nanoparticles assembled from photosensitizer molecules have attracted considerable attention the field of tumor phototherapies. However, most self-assembled photosensitizer nanoparticles only exhibit photothermal therapy (PTT) activity, while their photodynamic therapy (PDT) activity is greatly restricted. Here, we first obtained nanostructured ZnPc assemblies with dual-modal PTT and PDT functions upon exposure to monochromatic 650 nm near-infrared (NIR) irradiation. The as-prepared ZnPc nanoparticles not only showed good stability, excellent biocompatibility, and absorption in the appropriate NIR region, but also exhibited high photothermal conversion efficiency (as high as 31.3%) and remarkable PDT performance. The simple synthesis method and outstanding therapeutic effect from synergistic PTT and PDT make the as-obtained ZnPc nanoparticles an ideal phototheranostic agent for effective and safe cancer treatment in clinical research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnPc nanoparticles
650 nm laser
Photothermal therapy
Photodynamic therapy
Synergistic antitumor effect

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity of ZnPc NPs
In vivo photothermal imaging of ZnPc NPs
Synthesize and characterization of ZnPc NPs
The synergistic therapeutic performance of ZnPc nanoparticles in vitro
Cytotoxicity and cell viability to tumor cells.
In vivo anti-tumor efficiency of ZnPc NPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM and (b) TEM images of ZnPc NPs. (c) The DLS analysis of ZnPc NPs in aqueous solution. The inset presents Tyndall phenomenon of the ZnPc NPs. (d) The DLS stability of ZnPc NPs in water versus aging time. (e) The absorption spectra of ZnPc NPs in water and ZnPc in DMSO. (f) The fluorescence spectra of the ZnPc monomers (20 μM) in DMSO and ZnPc NPs (20 μM) in water excited by 650 nm laser.

(a) Infrared thermal images of ZnPc NPs aqueous solution (20 μM) irradiated with 650 nm laser for 11 min at the power densities of 0.7 W·cm−2. (b) Temperature change curves of ZnPc NPs solutions at varying ZnPc concentrations (0–20 μM, 2 mL) under laser irradiation (650 nm, 0.7 W·cm−2) as a function of the irradiation time. (c) Temperature increase (ΔT) of ZnPc NPs irradiated with 650 nm laser; the laser was turned off after irradiation for 600 s. (d) Obtained time constant for heat transfer of this system (τs = 373.71 s) by applying linear time data Vs − ln(θ) from the cooling stage. (e) The temperature variations of ZnPc NPs aqueous solution in water at 20 μM under 650 nm laser irradiation for 5 cycles. (f) Fluorescence spectra of DCFH solutions with ZnPc NPs at varying ZnPc concentrations (0–20 μM, 2 mL) under laser irradiation (650 nm, 0.7 W·cm−2) for 5 min. (g) Fluorescence spectra of DCFH solutions with ZnPc NPs at ZnPc concentrations (20 μM, 2 mL) under laser irradiation (650 nm, 0.7 W·cm−2) as a function of the irradiation time.

(a) Cell viability of ZnPc NPs incubated with the L929 cell line by the MTT assay. (b) Cell cytotoxicity of ZnPc NPs incubated with the Hela cell line by the MTT assay. (c) Hemolysis assay for ZnPc NPs (inset: photographic images for direct observation of hemolysis by ZnPc NPs using PBS as a negative control and water as a positive control (left two tubes), and ZnPc NPs suspensions with different concentrations). (d) CLSM image of HeLa cancer cells incubated with control, pure laser, pure ZnPc NPs (20 μM) and ZnPc NPs (20 μM) with different irradiation time. All the cells were dyed with Calcein AM and PI. All images share the same scale bar of 50 mm.

CLSM images of HeLa cervical cancer cells incubated with ZnPc NPs (20 μM) for 0.5 h, 1 h, and 3 h at 37 °C. For each panel, the images from left to right show cell nuclei stained by DAPI (left column), fluorescence images of ZnPc NPs (middle column) in dark field, overlays of the former two images (right column). The excitation wavelength of DAPI is 405 nm, respectively. All images share the same scale bar are 50 µm.

CLSM images of HeLa cancer cells after incubation with ZnPc NPs with different conditions. All group (control, pure laser, pure ZnPc NPs (20 μM) and ZnPc NPs (20 μM) with different irradiation time) were dealt with DCFH-DA for 30 min. All images share the same scale bar of 50 μm.

(a) Thermal imaging of U14 tumor-bearing mice before and after intravenous injection with saline (bottom) and ZnPc NPs (top) followed by 650 nm laser irradiation for 10 min. (b) Representative photographs of mice after 14 days’ treatment from different groups (including control, laser only, ZnPc NPs only, and ZnPc NPs with 650 nm laser irradiation); (c) Relative tumor growth curves and (d) Average tumor weight; (e) H&E stained slices of tumor tissues collected from different groups after treatment (including control, laser only, ZnPc NPs only, ZnPc NPs with 650 nm laser illumination). Error bars indicate standard deviations (***p < 0.001, **p < 0.01, or *p < 0.05, by the student’s post-test). All images share the same scale bar of 50 μm.

Representative histological H&E stained tissue sections from mice to monitor the histological change in heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney collected from different groups (control, laser only, ZnPc NPs only, ZnPc NPs with 650 nm laser illumination) followed by dissections after 14 days post-injection. All images share the same scale bar of 50 μm.
(a) Schematic illustration of the basic mechanism of PDT for ZnPc NPs, (b) self-assemble process of the ZnPc NPs.
Schematic illustration of the synergistic PTT and PDT therapeutic antitumor effect of self-assembled ZnPc NPs irradiated at 650 nm laser

head.ce:title: Self-assembled zinc phthalocyanine nanoparticles as excellent photothermal/photodynamic synergistic agent for antitumor treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Material Sciences and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology
College of Material Sciences and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Sciences, Heihe University, 164300, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Material Sciences and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology
College of Material Sciences and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional phototheranostic nanoparticles assembled from photosensitizer molecules have attracted considerable attention the field of tumor phototherapies. However, most self-assembled photosensitizer nanoparticles only exhibit photothermal therapy (PTT) activity, while their photodynamic therapy (PDT) activity is greatly restricted. Here, we first obtained nanostructured ZnPc assemblies with dual-modal PTT and PDT functions upon exposure to monochromatic 650 nm near-infrared (NIR) irradiation. The as-prepared ZnPc nanoparticles not only showed good stability, excellent biocompatibility, and absorption in the appropriate NIR region, but also exhibited high photothermal conversion efficiency (as high as 31.3%) and remarkable PDT performance. The simple synthesis method and outstanding therapeutic effect from synergistic PTT and PDT make the as-obtained ZnPc nanoparticles an ideal phototheranostic agent for effective and safe cancer treatment in clinical research.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, cancer is still a horrible disease that threatens human lives worldwide [1]. Improvement in efficacy of cancer treatment has attracted increasing research and scientific attention. Surgery, chemotherapy and radiation are the main therapeutic clinical methods, however, cancer cells and normal cells are simultaneously killed by the abovementioned measures, giving rise to new disease that may cause the death of the patients. Currently, PTT [2–5] and PDT [6–10] phototherapies are widely used in the clinical treatment of acne, skin cancer and other illnesses. PTT is a novel clinical treatment that uses the heat generated from the connection between the NIR light and the photothermal agent to raise the temperature at the tumor site and eliminate tumor cells [11–14]. The principles of ideal hyperthermia applied for the photothermal agent, include: (1) strong light absorption in the NIR region; (2) much higher photothermal conversion efficiency than those of traditional photothermal agents such as Au or CuS nanoparticles; (3) controllable functionalization and high tumor-targeting ability; (4) steady photostability, low toxicity, and excellent biodegradability [15–18]. PDT is another phototherapy that uses photogenerated reactive oxygen species (ROS) to kill tumor cells; in this case, the photosensitizer is aggregated at the tumor site by the EPR effect and produces ROS excited by specific laser irradiation [19–24]. Thus, designing such multifunctional therapeutic agents with high performance, high stability and good biocompatibility has become a hot spot of cancer phototherapy.

Phthalocyanines (Pcs) are four-pyrrole-rings complexes that possess an 18 π-electron conjugated planar structure [25]. Due to their long absorption wavelength (λmax > 660 nm), high extinction coefficients (εmax > 105 L·mol−1·cm−1) and photochemical properties, Pcs have been widely used as the second-generation photosensitizers for PDT [26–28]. Pcs absorb a photon excited by NIR laser irradiation and then transition from the ground state (S0) to the excited singlet state (S1) (Scheme 1 a). The deactivation of the S1 state is mainly due to either fluorescence emission or release of heat or to a transition to a lower energy excited triplet state (T1) by intersystem crossing (ISC). After the relaxation process from S1 to T1 through the emission of phosphorescence or the formation of the 1O2 generated by the excess energy of excited Pcs transferring from T1 to O2 in the cells, the excited electron in the T1 state is deactivated to the S0 state [29–32]. However, the poor solubility of Pcs is the bottleneck for PDT treatment in vivo. Therefore, these π-conjugated organic molecules substituted with the hydrophilic groups such as the hydroxyl, carboxyl and sulfo group have been widely used in phototherapy. The excellent PDT properties of the Pcs molecules can be transformed to an obvious PTT effect if the molecules would be prone to stack with other Pcs molecules by π–π interaction to form a nanostructure. The photothermal mechanism of phthalocyanine aggregates can be attributed to the suppression of the fluorescence emission from the S1 state to the S0 state and the intersystem crossing from the S1 to the T1 state, providing the main contribution to the PDT properties of Pcs [33–38]. Then, the unstable excited phthalocyanine aggregates will reach the stable state by releasing their excessive energy to produce heat that can be used in the PTT treatment to eliminate the tumors. To date, there have been few reports on the use of Pc nanostructures with high water-stability as PTT agents.

Recently, the self-assembly of porphyrin liposomes had been investigated in PTT by Zheng et al. [13,39,40] and the significant potential theranostic applications of porphyrin assembled nanodots were reported by Yan et al. [41]. Unlike the hematoporphyrins used in phototherapy, our Pcs could use peripheral substituents, axial ligands or central metal to change the photoproperties for imaging and phototherapy in biological tissues [42,43]. Our group has investigated the PDT properties of a series of composites that consist of a combination of upconversion nanoparticles and organic photosensitizer (Ce6 or nonsubstituted ZnPc) and realize the PDT function through the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process between the two moieties [44–47]. Based on the excellent phototherapy performance of Pcs and our previous phototherapy research, in the present work, we synthesized 1,8,15,22-tera-(3-(6-hydroxyl) hexyloxy) phthalocyanine zinc(II) (ZnPc) and constructed the nanostructure of Pcs by aggregation of Pc molecules. The obtained material not only exhibited high stability and good biocompatibility but also showed excellent PTT and PDT effects in vivo. Thus, our designed ZnPc nanoparticles are a promising cancer treatment agent.

All starting materials such nitrophthalonitrile, 1,6-hexanediol, dry potassium carbonate, and 1,8-diazabicyclo[5.4.0]undec-7-ene (DBU) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All of the solvents such as N, N-dimethylformamide, n-pentanol and dimethyl sulfoxide were obtained from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China. The above-mentioned solvent were freshly distilled under nitrogen atmosphere prior to use. 2,7-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCF), 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), Calcein AM, and propidium iodide were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., LLC.

The 3-(6-hydroxyl) hexyloxy phthalonitrile compound (0.8 mmol) was added into n-pentanol (20 mL). Then, anhydrous Zn(OAc)2 (0.05 mmol) and DBU (0.5 mL) were placed into the mixed solutions and refluxed for 12 h under nitrogen atmosphere. The reaction mixture was poured into ethyl acetate (20 mL), and then the sediment was separated from ethyl acetate. Then, the precipitate was purified by column chromatography (silica gel, dichloromethane–methanol mixture (15:1) as eluent). ZnPc Yield: 0.108 g (12.4%). 1H NMR (DMSO‑d 6 400 MHz) ppm 9.1–7.5 (m, 12H, aromatics), 4.7–1.2 (m, 48H, CH2), 5.1 (t, 4H, O–H). Elemental analysis (MW 369.3): C56H64N8O8Zn found (calcd): C 64.21% (64.52%); H 6.03% (6.19%); N 11.23% (10.75%); O 12.12% (12.28%); Zn 6.41% (6.27%). FT-IR [(KBr) νmax (cm−1)]: 3312 (OH), 2925 (CH), 1448 (CN). UV–vis (DMSO): λabs = 704 nm, 320 nm. MALDI-TOF-MS found (calcd): 1041.2 (1042.18).
To prepare ZnPc NPs, ZnPc (0.5 mg) and DMSO (100 μL) were added dropwise to H2O (1 mL). After 4 h of stirring, the ZnPc NPs were prepared by dialysis.

The samples were placed in 10 mm quartz cuvettes and their UV−vis absorbance spectra were measured using a UV-1601 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a Perkin Elmer instruments Spectrum One FT-IR spectrometer (KBr disks). 1H NMR spectra were recorded using a Bruker 400 MHz high-resolution mass spectra (HRMS) were determined using an Exactive Plus Orbitrap (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed using a Nano-ZS instrument (Malvern). SEM was performed using a JSM-6700F instrument (JEOL). TEM was performed using a JEM-2100F instrument (JEOL). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using an FLS980 device (Edinburgh instruments) at the excitation wavelength of 650 nm.

DCFH-DA has always been regarded as a chemical probe for the in vitro detection of the generated extracellular 1O2. Therefore, the fluorescent intensity of DCF oxidized from DCFH by 1O2 was measured using an FLS980 instrument excited at the 504 nm wavelength of the laser irradiation. First, the nonfluorescent DCFH was obtained which we mixed a methanol solution of DCFH-DA (0.5 mL, 1 mmol·L−1) and NaOH (2 mL, 0.01 mol·L−1). Second, the mixed solution was stirred for 0.5 h at room temperature in the dark. After the reaction was carried out for 30 min, sodium phosphate buffer (10 mL, PBS) was added to the DCFH solution to obtain neutral pH. Then, the mixed solution (4 mL) was placed into a 5 mL quartz tube that contained the aforementioned solution (2 mL) and ZnPc NPs solution (2 mL, 0, 2.5, 5, 10 and 20 μM). The five groups of mixed solutions were severally irradiated at 650 nm NIR laser for 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 min in the dark. Finally, the photoluminescence spectra of the abovementioned solutions were measured.

The cell viability of ZnPc NPs and the solvent was probed in a similar manner using the MTT method, with the L929 fibroblast cells used for the MTT assay. Briefly, the L929 cells were added to a 96-well plate (7000/well) with eight wells serving as normal control, and then the plate was placed into an incubator overnight in order to promote proliferation of the cultured cells at 37 °C in 5% CO2. Then, ZnPc NPs (100 μL) were injected into the plate, with the NPs prepared at different concentrations of 0, 2.5, 5, 10 and 20 μM. Furthermore, the cells are then cultivated for another 16 h in the incubator. Then, the as-prepared MTT (20 μL, 5 mg·mL−1) was added into each well and incubated at 37 °C for another 4 h. Afterwards, DMSO (100 μL) was added into each well and the 96-well plate was shaken for 15 min to form the formazan of the 96-well plate in which the formazan was measured using a Microplate reader at the wavelength of 490 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity of ZnPc NPs
ce:section.ce:para: First, the HeLa cells were cultivated in a 96-well plate (7000/well) and then placed in 5% CO2 at 37 °C overnight in order to allow the cells to grow along the wall of the well. Group 1 was the blank control group only without the laser irradiation and the ZnPc NPs, and group 2 was exposed to 650 nm laser light for 10 min. Then, the HeLa cells in the other four groups were incubated with different concentrations of ZnPc NPs (2.5, 5, 10 and 20 μM) for approximately 3 h and irradiated by a 650 nm laser for 30 min (groups 3–6). For all of these groups, the 650 nm laser intensity was 0.7 W·cm−2, and irradiation was applied for 10 min. After the cells of all of the groups continued to incubate for another 14 h in order to internalize the samples in the cells, the as-prepared MTT (20 μL, 5 mg·mL−1) was added into each well and incubated at 37 °C for another 4 h. Afterwards, DMSO (100 μL) was added into each well and then the 96-well plate was shaken for 15 min to form the formazan of 96-well plate, in which the formazan was measured using a Microplate reader at the wavelength of 490 nm. Finally, Calcein AM and propidium iodide (PI) methods were used to show dead (red) and live (green) cells for a more effective method for determining the cell viability.

The detection of hemolysis is an appropriate method for verifying the biocompatibility of the as-synthesized sample. First, the as-prepared red blood cells were centrifuged and washed with 0.9% saline five times. Second, the precipitated blood cells were dispersed in a PBS solution (10 mL). Then, the two groups of positive and negative control were prepared with the cells suspension (0.3 mL) extracted from the abovementioned PBS solution were mixed with deionized water and PBS (1.2 mL). The other four groups with cells suspensions (0.3 mL) were mixed with ZnPc NPs (1.2 mL) at various concentrations (2.5, 5, 10 and 20 μM). Third, the six groups were set and kept stable for 3 h at room temperature. Finally, the six groups of the mixtures were centrifuged, and the supernatant liquids were obtained and used to measure the UV–vis absorbance spectra. The hemolysis percentage was calculated according to following formula:
(1)
Hemolysis
% =
A sample
-
A
control ( - )
/
A
control ( + )
-
A
control ( - )
where A is the absorbance obtained from the UV−vis spectrum.

The HeLa cells were incubated with ZnPc NPs (20 µM) for 2 h, and then DCF (100 μL, 5 µM) was added to the cells and kept at 15 min. After this procedure, the cells were washed with PBS and irradiated with a 650 nm laser at 0.7 W·cm−2 for 5 min. Ultimately, 1O2 images of cells were taken using a confocal laser scanning microscope with the 1O2 probe excited at 488 nm.

Twenty female Kunming mice (20–25 g) that were purchased from Harbin Veterinary Research Institute were randomly divided into four groups (5 mice per group). First, the four groups of mice were separately given a subcutaneous injection of the U14 tumor cells into the left axilla in order for the cells to proliferate and form tumors. All four groups of tumor-bearing mice were treated by intratumoral injection after the tumors reached the volume of 100 mm3. The four groups were separated, with one group treated by intratumoral injection with ZnPc NPs with 650 nm NIR irradiation, one with ZnPc NPs, one with 650 nm NIR irradiation only, and the last group served as the control group. The control group without any treatment was used as the blank control. 200 μL of NPs (20 µM) were used for the group injected with ZnPc NPs and the group injected with ZnPc NPs with 650 nm NIR irradiation. The tumor site of the groups that were exposed to 650 nm NIR laser was irradiated for 10 min, except the control group and the group injected with ZnPc NPs in the dark. After treatment, the size of the tumor was measured every day using a Vernier caliper. Since the four groups of mice completed the treatment, the average tumor volumes were calculated every 2 days until twenty mice were kept for 14 days. The diameters of the tumors were calculated, and the tumor width and length were obtained every day using a Vernier caliper and were calculated according to:
(2)
V = L ×
W 2
/ 2
where V, L and W are the volume, length and width of the tumor, respectively. The Relative tumor volume was calculated as V/V 0 (V 0 is the initial tumor volume of the mice).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo photothermal imaging of ZnPc NPs
ce:section.ce:para: U14 tumor cells were introduced into the left armpit of the four groups of female Kunming mice by intratumoral injection. After the average tumor sizes approached approximately 100 mm3, ZnPc NPs (400 μL, 20 µM) were injected into two groups. After the injection of the nanoparticles, thermal images were obtained using an R300SR-HD infrared camera for 1–10 min as the tumor sites were exposed to 650 nm NIR laser irradiation.

The photographs of different main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) dissected from the mice in different treatment groups were analyzed by Histology analysis after 14 days of treatment. The dehydration processing of the organs was carried out as follows: buffered formalin, different concentrations of ethanol solution, and xylene were added sequentially. Then, the dehydrated organs were placed in liquid paraffin. Finally, the different groups of main tissues and tumor were sliced and then stained with H&E. Finally, digital photos of the organs were taken using a Leica TCS SP8 camera.

The collected statistical data were shown as the values ± S.D. The statistical data was obtained from three samples and calculated by Student’s t-test. The data were regarded as statistically significant for *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesize and characterization of ZnPc NPs
ce:section.ce:para: First, the synergistic therapeutic ZnPc NPs were fabricated by the self-assembly process, as illustrated in Scheme 1b. The structures of the precursor ZnPc and phthalocyanine ZnPc that had four peripheral hydrophilic long chain hydroxy substituents were synthesized and characterized by MALDI-TOF mass and 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S1–2). The critical micelle concentration of the ZnPc NPs was obtained from the DLS spectra. The molecular assemblies were formed by controlling the concentration of ZnPc as shown in Fig. S4. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, the SEM and TEM images of ZnPc NPs exhibited nearly a regular spherical shape with a diameter of approximately 50 nm. A proper nanosized dispersion of ZnPc NPs was realized by controlling the concentration of ZnPc and the ratio of the solvents (DMSO and H2O). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) results presented in Fig. 1c revealed that the size of ZnPc NPs was slightly larger (nearly 70 nm) than that measured from the TEM image, which was probably caused by the shrinkage of the micelles during the drying process of the TEM sample [49–51]. The inset of Fig. 1cshowed that the dispersion of ZnPc NPs induced the Tyndall phenomenon, indicating that the solution contained particles with the size of less than 100 nm. Even though ZnPc assemblies in water were preserved for 48 h, the size of ZnPc NPs was almost unchanged, as shown in Fig. 1d. As shown in Fig. S5a, the size (almost 70 nm) of ZnPc NPs with the concentration of 20 μM was slightly larger than those of the ZnPc NPs with other concentrations when the solutions were stored for 0.5 h. Similarly, the size of ZnPc NPs in water changed little even if the ZnPc NPs solution was placed into water for 48 h. The results demonstrated the excellent stability of the ZnPc NPs solution.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of ZnPc monomer and ZnPc NPs are shown in Fig. 1e. It is observed that the ZnPc monomer spectrum displayed the characteristic absorption of metal phthalocyanines (MPcs) with an intense S0-S1 transition band (Q band) followed by a smaller shoulder and a weak broad Soret band at 300–420 nm [52]. According to Kasha’s exciton coupling theory, the blueshift of the Q band and Soret band for ZnPc NPs relative to those of the ZnPc monomer is due to the face-to-face molecular stacking in their nanostructures [53,54], and H-aggregates [55]. The fluorescence emission spectra of ZnPc NPs and ZnPc molecule are shown in Fig. 1f, where ZnPc NPs were dispersed in water with the same concentration as that of the ZnPc dispersed in DMSO. After 2 days' dialysis, the residual molecular ZnPcs could be almost diffused into the outer solvent by using the concentration gradient between inner and outer solution (Fig. S6). Different from the strong emission of the ZnPc monomer, fluorescence quenching was observed for ZnPc NPs in water due to the suppressed fluorescence emission and intersystem crossing effect of the H-aggregates [56,57]. Even though the fluorescent intensity of ZnPc NPs was only 10% of that for ZnPc, the clearly observed emission means that the nanoparticles should still exhibit the PDT effect. To examine their suitability for biological applications, the stabilities of ZnPc NPs were tested by dispersing in respective PBS and DMEM, as shown in Fig. S7a and b. After 48 h, all of the ZnPc NPs with different concentrations in PBS and DHEM showed excellent stability. As discussed above, ZnPc NPs not only exhibited an appropriate size for biomedical applications, excellent dispersivity and stability in water for phototherapy but also had potential PTT and PDT effect due to the combination of their UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synergistic therapeutic performance of ZnPc nanoparticles in vitro
ce:section.ce:para:
The photothermal effect of ZnPc NPs was evaluated for various ZnPc concentrations (0–20 μM) excited at 650 nm laser (0.7 W·cm−2, 11 min). The infrared thermal images of ZnPc NPs in vitro with the concentration of 20 μM are shown in Fig. 2 a. After exposure to the 650 nm laser for 11 min, the temperature of ZnPc NPs reached 53.5 °C. The plot of the temperature versus the particle concentrations after 11 min of irradiation is presented in Fig. 2b, showing the highest temperature of ZnPc NPs (20 μM). As mentioned above, the heat was generated by ZnPc NPs when the sample was excited by NIR laser and the higher concentration of the ZnPc NPs had a better PTT effect. Then, the photothermal effect of the ZnPc NPs could be investigated in-depth by measuring the photothermal conversion efficiency η (PTCE), and η was calculated according to the data presented in Fig. 2c and d according to the Eqs. (3) and (4).
(3)
η =
h A Δ
T max
-
Q s
I
1 -
10
-
A λ
(4)
hA =
mC
τ s
where h is the heat transfer coefficient, S is the surface area of the container, m is the mass of the ZnPc NPs solution (m = 0.5 g), Qs was measured to be 35.8 J/s, τs is the system time constant of 373.71 s, Cwater is the specific heat capacity of the solvent (Cwater = 4.2 J/g·°C), I is the incident laser power (0.7 W·cm−2), η is the photothermal conversion efficiency, A is the absorbance of ZnPc NPs (A 650), and ΔTmax is the temperature difference between the maximum temperature of ZnPc NPs and the ambient temperature.

It was found that ZnPc NPs exhibited the η value of 31.3%, which was clearly higher than those of the conventional photothermal agents such as CuS nanocrystals (22%), Au nanorods (21%), black phosphorus (28%), and graphene oxide (25%) [58–61]. The photothermal conversion efficiency of ZnPc NPs still remained high and was almost unchanged after five on-off cycles of the exposure to the NIR laser (Fig. 2e), indicating their excellent photostability. ZnPc NPs acted as an excellent PTT agent for anticancer therapy due to their highly efficient photothermal conversion efficiency and long-term photothermal stability.

Due to the incomplete fluorescence quenching, the photodynamic properties of ZnPc NPs could be deduced from the analysis of the fluorescence results presented in Fig. 1f. The singlet oxygen (1O2) generated by PDT damaged the mitochondria and DNA of the tumor cells, thus achieving the aim of cancer treatment. The concentration of extracellular 1O2 was measured by the fluorescent intensity of dichlorofluorescein (DCF) that was obtained from nonfluorescent DCFH by oxidation of 1O2. When DCFH reacted with the 1O2 generated from the ZnPc NPs excited with 650 nm laser irradiation (0.7 W·cm−2), the intensity of the fluorescent emission peak of DCF at 520–540 nm increased remarkably. The emission intensities of DCF versus the particle concentrations and the irradiation time are presented in Fig. 2f and g. As shown in Fig. 2f, lower concentrations of ZnPc NPs corresponded to lower emission intensities for the same irradiation time, indicating that ZnPc NPs at the concentration of 20 μM showed the best PDT effect. With the gradually prolonged laser exposure time, the fluorescent peak of DCF mixed with ZnPc NPs at the concentration of 20 μM increased significantly (Fig. 2g). The singlet oxygen generation efficiency of ZnPc in the free molecule and nanoparticle forms were respectively calculated according to Eq. (5) [62].
(5)
ϕ Δ
=
k
sample
/
k
nonsubstituted
Z n P c
A
nonsubstituted
Z n P c
/
A
sample
×
ϕ
Δ n o n s u b s t i t u t e d
Z n P c
where k is the initial rate of the reaction of DPBF with 1O2 in the presence of a photosensitizer (non-substituted ZnPc, ZnPc molecules or ZnPc NPs). The value of k was obtained from Fig. S8a and b. A is the photosensitizer (non-subsitituted ZnPc, ZnPc molecules or ZnPc NPs) absorbance at 650 nm. ϕ Δnon-subsitituted ZnPc was found to be 0.6. The value of ϕ ΔZnPc molecules was estimated to be 0.62 and the value of ϕ ΔZnPc NPs was regarded as 0.24, suggesting a clear difference in the 1O2 production rate between the ZnPc molecules and ZnPc NPs. Through the above analysis, it was inferred that ZnPc NPs still generated 1O2 under the 650 nm laser irradiation. Accordingly, the ZnPc NPs are promising potential candidate material for PDT/PTT synergistic anticancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and cell viability to tumor cells.
ce:section.ce:para: The PTT and PDT effects of ZnPc NPs were examined to determine whether this material can be applied for synergistic PTT and PDT in vitro. First, the biocompatibility, cytotoxicity and hemolysis were measured, with the results of the measurements presented in Fig. 3a–c. The biocompatibility of ZnPc NPs in vitro was evaluated using L929 cells. The rate of cell viability was maintained at over 90% after treatment with different concentrations of ZnPc NPs or the reactive solvents (Fig. 3a), implying that the ZnPc NPs had low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Then, the cytotoxicity of ZnPc NPs was measured using HeLa cells (Fig. 3b). The viability of the HeLa cells decreased with higher concentration of the NPs irradiated by 650 nm NIR laser, and little cytotoxicity was detected for 650 nm laser irradiation only. In particular, more than 93% of the HeLa cells were eliminated by the synergistic PTT and PDT for the particle concentration of 20 μM upon laser exposure for 10 min. To further demonstrate the more advantageous synergistic effect of ZnPc NPs, the effects of PDT alone and PTT alone serving as contrasts were determined by using inhibitors or controlling the experiment conditions. Vitamin C has numerous health benefits, in particular the elimination of ROS generated from our cells [63]. When vitamin C (40 µM, 100 µL) was mixed into the ZnPc NPs solution, the mixture only exhibited the PTT effect under 650 nm laser irradiation. In contrast to the inhibitor PDT effect of vitamin C, the PTT was inhibited in an ice bath to control the temperature of the cells to be less than 37 °C [53]. The cells that were treated with pure vitamin C or were placed in an ice bath showed survival of more than 96%, indicating that vitamin C or the temperature did not damage the cells. Compared to the synergistic treatment of the ZnPc NPs irradiated by 650 nm laser, the single effects (PDT or PTT) exhibited lower therapeutic effectiveness (Fig. S9a and b). Fig. 3 c shows the hemolytic results and digital photographs of the ZnPc NPs solutions with different concentrations (2.5–20 μM). The mixtures of PBS and red blood cells (RBCs) served as negative control and positive control, respectively. The high hemolytic efficiency of ZnPc NPs (0.049%) was far below the standard value (5%). suggesting negligible hemolysis. The cell-killing efficiency was verified by the Calcein AM and PI method, where the living cells were incubated by AM with a green color and the dead cells were dyed by PI with a red color. As shown in Fig. 3d, almost all of the HeLa cells treated with ZnPc NPs (20 μM) and laser irradiation only were alive, indicating that the particles and laser irradiation only also had a negligible negative effect on the cells. When the HeLa cells were treated with ZnPc NPs exposed to 650 nm laser for 1 min, the HeLa cells began to die. After irradiation for 5 min, almost 10% of the cells had been killed. After exposure to NIR laser irradiation for 10 min, almost no alive HeLa cells were found due to the synergistic PTT and PDT anticancer effect. At the same time, the Calcein AM and PI method was also used to demonstrate that the synergistic effect of ZnPc NPs was more advantageous than the single PDT and PTT effects (Fig. S9c). The inhibitory effect found by the AM/PI assay was in agreement with the results of the cytotoxicity experiment described above.

The cellular uptake ability of the ZnPc NPs consumed by HeLa cells for different times (0.5 h, 1 h and 3 h) in the CO2 incubator was investigated and observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence signal of ZnPc NPs incubated for 3 h was much stronger than those of the ZnPc NPs incubated for 0.5 h and 1 h, suggesting that ZnPc NPs were consumed by HeLa cells for a period of time.

The generation of intracellular 1O2 was detected using the DCFH-DA fluorescent dye that can identify the PDT effect of ZnPc NPs. As shown in Fig. 5 , the intracellular PDT processed occurred when the ZnPc NPs (20 μM) swallowed by the cancer cells were subjected to laser irradiation. In the PDT process, some ZnPc molecules of the nanoparticles first absorbed photons provided by the 650 nm laser and transition to the unstable S1 state. Then, the excited molecules in the S1 state transitioned to the T1 state through intersystem crossing. Finally, the excited molecules transferred their excess energy to molecular oxygen to generate 1O2 and decayed back to the S0 state. With the increase in the irradiation time, the green fluorescent DCF intensity of the ZnPc NPs group with laser irradiation was much stronger than those for the groups without any treatment or with laser irradiation only (Fig. 5). At the same time, vitamin C (40 µM, 100 µL) was injected into the one group of HeLa cells that had swallowed ZnPc NPs. Then, the cells were irradiated under 650 nm laser for 5 min. Even though the irradiation time was increased, green fluorescence was not detected because vitamin C inhibited the 1O2 generated from ZnPc NPs, illustrating that the green fluorescence originated from DCF oxidized by 1O2 (Fig. S10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo anti-tumor efficiency of ZnPc NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the excellent photothermal performance in vitro, the photothermal experiment of ZnPc NPs in various concentrations was cultivated in the wells where HeLa cells were bred. The temperature of ZnPc NPs at the concentration of 20 μM under 650 nm laser irradiation for 10 min was much higher than the temperature of the wells cultivated with other concentrations of ZnPc NPs (Fig. S11), indicating that ZnPc NPs acted as a photothermal agent in vivo. Fig. 6 a shows the real-time infrared thermal images of tumor-bearing mice for which ZnPc NPs with the concentration of 20 μM and pure PBS were respectively injected into the left axilla of mice via an intratumoral injection and then were illuminated with 650 nm NIR laser (0.7 W·cm−2) for 10 min. It is observed that the temperature of the tumor-bearing mice injected with 20 μM ZnPc NPs reached 53.2 °C after exposure for 10 min, which was much higher than the temperature (38.8 °C) of the control group (saline + NIR laser) (Fig. S12). This was due to the better photothermal conversion efficiency and stronger absorption of the product at 650 nm NIR-region, in agreement with the results of the in vitro photothermal measurements. The direct phototherapeutic performance of ZnPc NPs in vivo was illustrated from the curves of the relative change of the tumor volume. The weight of the tumor and the digital photographs of the mice after treatment are displayed in Fig. 6b–d. It is observed from Fig. 6b, that the tumor-bearing mice that were injected by ZnPc NPs solution (20 μM) and exposed to 650 nm laser irradiation for 10 min exhibit an obvious decrease in the tumor size after the treatment because of the combined PTT and PDT effect. For the control groups injected with saline after 14 days, the size of tumors was much larger than that of the group injected with ZnPc NPs under 650 nm laser irradiation. The groups with laser irradiation only and injection with ZnPc NPs only both showed a slight inhibition of the tumor growth in the first few days, that was then followed by the rapid growth of the tumor with longer treatment time, indicating that the ZnPc NPs alone and laser irradiation alone could not effectively inhibit tumor growth. Compared to the single PDT or PTT effects, the combination of two therapies based on the ZnPc NPs obtained excellent tumor treatment efficacy. Nevertheless, the single therapy still exhibited antitumor performance in Fig. S13, the performance of them also was lower than the therapeutic synergistic effect of ZnPc NPs, their performance was also lower than the therapeutic synergistic effect of the ZnPc NPs. The tumor weights of the mice from the four groups were kept for 14 days and the corresponding photographs of the tumors are presented in Fig. 6c and 6d, respectively. As observed from the figures, ZnPc NPs gave rise to apparent photodamage of the tumor exposed to laser irradiation and prevented tumor repropagation after the 14 days of treatment. In particular, the tumor size of the mice that were injected with ZnPc NPs and were subjected to 650 nm laser irradiation for 10 min was much smaller than that of the control group.

As a further evidence, the synergistic therapeutic efficacy of ZnPc NPs was further analyzed and evaluated by Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining as shown in Fig. 6e. The group treated with ZnPc NPs irradiated by 650 nm NIR laser exhibited a clear inhibition effect of tumor growth and the tumor size was much smaller than that of the other groups. Meanwhile, more tumor cells of the group showed necrosis compared to the little damage observed for the tumor cells in other groups, indicating good agreement with the tumor growth results. The results of the histology analysis of the main organs including the spleen, lung, heart, liver, and kidneys from the mice after 14 days treatment with different therapeutic methods are provided in Fig. 7 . It is observed that the photos of the organs of the four groups of mice did not show obvious destruction of the main organs, suggesting that the ZnPc NPs possessed remarkable biocompatibility in vivo. All of the above synergistic PTT/PDT therapeutic performance characteristics are schematically illustrated in Scheme 2 . Therefore, ZnPc NPs, with excellent biocompatibility and synergistic therapeutic effects, are a suitable candidate material for clinical phototherapy applications.

In summary, the ZnPc NPs with combined PTT and PDT effects were successfully synthesized, and the corresponding applications in vitro and in vivo were investigated. Under 650 nm NIR irradiation, the ZnPc NPs not only showed an excellent PTT effect obtained by the aggregation of Pcs molecules but also still had the PDT function of tumor cell elimination. It was found that the ZnPc NPs exhibited high biocompatibility, good stability and excellent therapeutic performance in vivo. Based on the above findings, the ZnPc NPs can be regarded as an excellent, low-toxicity and highly efficient PTT/PDT synergistic therapeutic agent for antitumor activity in clinical research.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 51772059, 51602072, 51702070, 51602073, and 51502050), Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (QC2017006), a General Financial Grant from the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M600241), a Special Financial Grant from the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2017T00228), Harbin Sci.-Tech. Innovation Foundation (2016RAXXJ005, 2017RAQXJ077), and the Fundamental Research funds for the Central Universities are greatly acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2018.12.007.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
